Standard Document

Twelfth Edition

Microbiology

### CLSI M07

#### Methods for Dilution Antimicrobial Susceptibility Tests for Bacteria That Grow Aerobically

CLSI M07 provides essential guidance for antimicrobial susceptibility testing of aerobic bacteria using broth macrodilution, broth microdilution, and agar dilution methods. This standard is crucial for determining the susceptibility of organisms involved in infectious processes when resistance patterns cannot be reliably predicted based on species identity.

Designed for laboratories performing susceptibility testing, M07 ensures accurate and standardized minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) determinations. Every purchase includes the M07-Ed12 Quick Guide for streamlined reference. Discounted package pricing is available when purchasing the latest editions of CLSI M02, M07, and M100 together.

March 19, 2024

Barbara L. Zimmer, PhD

### {{FormatPrice(currentPrice)}}

### Free

{{FormatPrice(nonMemberPrice)}} List Price

This is your member pricing.

{{props.option.format}}

[Notify Me About New Editions](/new-edition-alerts?new_product_notify=m07)

Abstract

Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute M07—Methods for Dilution Antimicrobial Susceptibility Tests for Bacteria That Grow Aerobically offers guidance for antimicrobial susceptibility testing that is indicated for any organism that contributes to an infectious process warranting antimicrobial chemotherapy, if its susceptibility cannot be reliably predicted from knowledge of the organism’s identity. Susceptibility tests are most often indicated when the causative organism is thought to belong to a species capable of exhibiting resistance to commonly used antimicrobial agents. Various laboratory methods can be used to measure the in vitro susceptibility of bacteria to antimicrobial agents. Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute M07—Methods for Dilution Antimicrobial Susceptibility Tests for Bacteria That Grow Aerobically describes standard broth dilution (macrodilution and microdilution [the microdilution method described in CLSI M07 is the same methodology outlined in ISO 20776-1])1 and agar dilution techniques, and it includes a series of procedures to standardize the way the tests are performed. The performance, applications, and limitations of the current CLSI-recommended methods are also described. The supplemental information (CLSI M1002 tables) used with this standard represents the most current information for drug selection, interpretation, and QC using the procedures standardized in CLSI M07.

Overview of Changes

This standard replaces CLSI M07-Ed11, published in 2018, with several updates. General changes include revised information for testing and reporting, updated nomenclature from “coagulase-negative staphylococci” (CoNS) to “staphylococci other than Staphylococcus aureus” (SOSA), and changes in terminology from “groups” to “tiers” and “microdilution trays” to “microdilution panels.” Definitions and abbreviations have been updated, with new definitions added and some deleted. Subchapters on selecting antimicrobial agents, equivalent agents, and suggested guidelines for testing and reporting have been clarified and updated, including the addition of new tables for antimicrobial agent tiers and designations. New information on preparing and storing diluted antimicrobial agents, inoculum preparation, and inoculation methods has been included, along with updates to agar dilution procedures and special considerations for fastidious organisms and resistance detection.

Additional changes include the addition of iron-depleted cation-adjusted Mueller-Hinton broth for testing cefiderocol and Mueller-Hinton fastidious broth for fastidious organisms. The document now includes step-action tables for colony counts and new subchapters on interpreting results and determining end points. Special considerations for detecting resistance have been revised, with new information on mecC isolates, vancomycin-resistant and vancomycin-intermediate S. aureus, AmpC enzymes, and carbapenemases. The standard also provides recommendations for selecting quality control strains and updates to conditions for dilution antimicrobial susceptibility tests, including new acceptable media for nonfastidious and fastidious organisms.

Scope

This standard describes standard broth (macrodilution and microdilution) and agar dilution methods for determining in vitro susceptibility to antimicrobial agents for bacteria that grow aerobically and includes:

• Broth and agar dilution test preparation

• Testing conditions, including inoculum preparation and standardization, incubation time, and incubation temperature

• Results interpretation

• QC procedures

• Dilution test method limitations

To assist the medical laboratory, recommendations are provided for selecting antimicrobial agents for routine testing and reporting. Standards for testing the in vitro antimicrobial susceptibility of bacteria that grow aerobically using the antimicrobial disk testing method are found in CLSI M02. Standards for testing the in vitro antimicrobial susceptibility of bacteria that grow anaerobically are found in CLSI M11. Guidelines for standardized antimicrobial susceptibility testing (AST) of infrequently isolated or fastidious bacteria that are not included in CLSI M02, M07, or M11 are available in CLSI M45.

The AST methods provided in this standard can be used in laboratories around the world, including but not limited to:

• Medical laboratories

• Public health laboratories

• Research laboratories

• Food laboratories

• Environmental laboratories

results.raw_content:
SCACM Workshop: Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing Session I: AST Nuts and Bolts Matthew England, PhD, D(ABMM) Director, Microbiology Summit Pathology Associates Summa Health System, Akron, OH Andrew Clark, MS, PhD, D(ABMM) Assistant Professor, Pathology Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine Director, Bacteriology Laboratory The Johns Hopkins Hospital, Baltimore, MD 2025 SCACM Spring Meeting Wednesday March 26th, 2025 Sandusky OH Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing: Workshop Overview Session I: AST Nuts and Bolts Objectives 1. Discuss theory, mechanisms, and interpretation of routine AST methods 2. Understand how to approach AST technical issues 3. Learn to recognize abnormal AST results and how to address them This interactive workshop will utilize outside resources freely available online Participants are encouraged to utilize cell phones/laptops/tablets to access these resources during this workshop to maximize participation and comprehension 2 Two Key Questions: 1.
What organism is causing the infection? – i.e. Etiology 2.
What antimicrobials can we use to treat the infection? – i.e. Management Etiology Management Microbiologic Evaluation of Clinical Specimens What are we doing here?!
Identification/Susceptibility testing (when needed) guides therapeutic intervention There are multiple steps for translation of AST data to actionable results for management Receipt Culture Susceptibility Testing (if needed) Treatment Reading Standardization Interpretation Troubleshooting Reporting Quality Assessment Identification 3 Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing Do you need more information?
4 20-year-old male presents with fever, dysphagia, odynophagia and left neck swelling. CT reveals a peritonsillar abscess, and a bedside I/D was performed by ENT.
Empiric antibiotics (amoxacillin/clavulante) were provided, and purulent material was submitted to the microbiology laboratory for culture.
Gram stain of the submitted material revealed Gram-positive cocci in chains, and a beta-hemolytic, PYR-positive, catalase-negative organism was recovered.
Do you need additional susceptibilities? Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing Do you need more information?
5 Beta-hemolytic streptococci have perceptible susceptibility profile Barring an unforeseen complicating factor, no additional susceptibility testing is needed What if instead this was a leg wound with a Gram stain that revealed abundant Gram-positive cocci in clusters, which subsequently grew out a beta-hemolytic, catalase-positive, Staphaurex positive organism?
Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing Do you need more information?
6 Yes! – S. aureus susceptibilities are not predictable Susceptibility testing is needed to guide therapeutic management If considering a beta-lactam agent: MRSA – resistant to most beta-lactam agents – vancomycin or other drug class needed If patient is on vancomycin and isolate comes back as methicillin-susceptible, de-escalation to a beta-lactam (nafcillin) is needed If considering a drug from a different class: S. aureus does not exhibit predictable susceptibility profiles for other clinically active drugs including oral options like tetracyclines and bactrim The decision to get AST is multifactorial based on the attributes of both the host and the pathogen Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing What are we doing here?!
7 Why do we test?
Some isolates can be treated predictably (empirically) – Beta-hemolytic streptococci Some isolates are treatable but become resistant – Enterobacterales Some isolates have significant intrinsic resistance – Acinetobacter, Pseudomonas, Stenotrophomonas Important factors impacting antibiotic selection Sterile site vs. non-sterile Source of infection Superficial vs. deep Penetration issues Anatomic site of infection Intrinsic resistance Organism PO, IM, IV Route of administration Bacteriostatic vs. bactericidal Mechanism of action Allergies, drug-drug interactions Contraindications Patient compliance Cost Goal: the right drug, for the right patient, for the right amount of time Q: “We need a PO medication” Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing Why is the doctor bothering me?
Miscellaneous Tetracycline Fluoroquinolone Macrolide + Friends Beta-Lactam Trim/Sulfa Tetracycline Ciprofloxacin Azithromycin Penicillin Linezolid Doxycycline Levofloxacin Erythromycin Amoxicillin Nitrofurantoin Minocycline Moxifloxacin Clindamycin Amox/Clav Fosfomycin Cephalexin Cefuroxime Cefdinir Cefpodoxime Cost Dosing Linezolid ($) vs Tedizolid ($$$) Ertapenem (q24h) vs Meropenem (q8h) 8 Basic Concepts: Activities of Antimicrobial Agents The activity of an agent impacts drug selection for patient management Antibiotic activity is linked to a cellular target 9 Time Number of Bacteria Bacteriostatic Bactericidal + Drug - Drug Bactericidal Bacteriostatic Beta-lactams Macrolides Aminoglycosides Sulfonamides Nitroimidazoles Chloramphenicol Glycopeptides Oxazolidinones Quinolones Lincosamides Rifampin Tetracyclines Polymyxins Prevent bacterial from multiplying Kill the bacteria Protein Synthesis Cell wall synthesis DNA synthesis Others Basic Concepts: Antibiotic Targets and Resistance 10 Resistance arises through varied mechanisms: 1. Efflux 2. Impermeability 3. Target modification 4. Enzymatic inactivation Resistance can be acquired or intrinsic Resistance can be inducible or constitutive Combinations can lead to: 1.
resistance to different drug classes and/or 2.
tolerance to higher compound levels https://www.clinicaleducation.org/resources/reviews/antibiotics-how-do-they-work/ Goal of AST: phenotypically detect these events to direct therapeutic selection Basic Concepts: AST Modalities What methods can I use to report susceptibility testing data?
11 AST methods exhibit assay-specific characteristics and unique technical requirements Despite these differences, there are many commonalities Common features of AST methods Requirement for isolated, pure microorganisms Normalization of growth to a defined amount (McFarland standards) Log phase growth (18–24-hour culture) Specific growth media with defined composition (pH, cations, thymidine) Defined incubation duration and conditions Defined QC methods with recommended organisms exhibiting predetermined resistance phenotypes Basic Concepts: AST Modalities What methods can I use to report susceptibility testing data?
12 Methods that can be used to report AST data: 1.
CLSI reference AST methods including those in the M100 and M07 – Broth dilution MIC, Agar dilution 2.
Kirby Bauer Disk Diffusion Method as described in CLSI M02 3.
FDA-cleared device which has demonstrated comparability to an established CLSI reference method CLSI reference methods can be used for: – Routine reporting of susceptibility of patient isolates – Evaluation of commercial devices by laboratories or drug manufacturers – Surveillance of antimicrobial resistance Basic Concepts: AST Modalities Overview of Manual Methods 13 Endpoint Susceptibility Test Assay Type MIC Agar Dilution Dilution Gradient MIC Broth Dilution MIC Gradient Diffusion Diffusion Gradient ZOI Disc Diffusion Dilution Gradient Methods Assays utilize serial dilutions of defined concentrations of antibiotics Minimal Inhibitory Concentration (MIC) Broth dilution, Agar dilution Diffusion Gradient Methods Fixed concentration of antibiotic diffuses through media forming a gradient Inferred MIC or Zone of inhibition Kirby-Bauer, Etest, MTS strip Basic Concepts: AST Modalities Overview of Manual Dilution Methods 14 Agar Dilution (CLSI Reference Method) Solid agar plates with fixed concentrations of a single antibiotic spanning a dilution range are poured and spotted with a standardized inoculum – Multiple strains tested against a single drug Broth Dilution (CLSI Reference Method) Standardized inoculum in liquid media used to test susceptibility against multiple antibiotics – Single strain tested against multiple drugs – ISO 20776-1 Meropenem µg/mL 0.5 1 2 4 1. Prepare inoculum 2. Load wells 3. Stamp Steers Replicator Broth microdilution (mLs) Broth microdilution (≤100 µL) Broth Microdilution (BMD) Assay Overview and Considerations 15 Adherence to standardized practices facilitates reproducibility Methods developed for standardization of: – Antimicrobial agent potency – Test Media (supplementation, pH, depth, etc.) – Inoculum – Incubation conditions – Endpoint reading Useful References: CLSI M07, EUCAST website, ISO 20776 CLSI Recommendations for routine BMD of non-fastidious aerobic bacteria (M07) 0.5 McFarland initial preparation Final concentration: 5x105 cfu/mL Colony suspension or broth culture Inoculum CAMHB +/- modifications (based on drug) Media 35°C (+/- 2°C) for 16-20 hours May vary for different organisms Incubation MIC Adjust for trailing endpoints, 80% reduction of growth in a drug-specific manner Reading CLSI M07 ED 12 (2024) document Broth Microdilution (BMD) Media Selection 16 Cation-adjusted Mueller-Hinton broth – Good reproducibility, few inhibitors, most data – Supports growth of most pathogens Modifications to CAMHB for certain drug/bug combinations – +2% NaCl for oxacillin/staphylococci – + 0.002% Tween-80 for dalbavancin, oritavancin, telavancin – Additional calcium = 50µg/mL for daptomycin – Iron-depleted CAMHB supplemented with 20-25 µg/mL Ca, 10-12.5 µg/mL Mg, and 0.5-1 µg/mL Zn for cefiderocol CLSI M07 ED 12 (2024) document Medias Utilized for BMD Cation-adjusted Mueller-Hinton Broth Routine non-fastidious AST CAMHB CAMHB + 2.5-5% Lysed Horse Blood Streptococci CAMHB + 2.5-5% LHB Haemophilus Test Medium H. influenzae/Haemophilus parainfluenzae HTM Mueller-Hinton Fastidious Broth H. influenzae MH-F Brucella broth with hemin, vitamin K, and 5% Laked Sheep’s Blood Anaerobes: B. fragilis/B. thetaiotaomicron only BB + hemin, vit K, LSB Broth Microdilution (BMD) Panel preparation 17 CLSI M07 ED 12 (2024) document 2-fold dilutions of 10X antimicrobial stock created in broth or sterile water – Diluent choice impacted by drug solubility Dispense 50 µL of agent into wells at 2X the desired final concentration – A growth control well with no antibiotic should always be included Seal panels in plastic bags and store at ≤-20°C (preferably ≤-60°C) – Usually good for several months – Certain agents are less stable(imipenem, clavulanate) Broth Microdilution (BMD) and Agar Dilution (AD) Inoculum preparation 18 Colony suspension method used by most labs 3-4 well-isolated colonies from 18–24-hour culture on non-selective media – Saline, sterile water, TSB – Blood agar: Routine – Chocolate agar: Haemophilus sp., N. meningitidis – Sheep’s blood agar: Streptococcus sp.
Suspension adjusted to 0.5 McFarland* Inoculate plates within 15 minutes CLSI M07 ED 12 (2024) document Q: Why is it important to utilize >1 colony from a given isolate when preparing AST inoculum?
0.25 0.5 1 2 4 K. pneumoniae Ceftriaxone MIC = 1µg/mL S MIC = 4µg/mL R Loss of resistance determinants can occur (i.e. plasmid loss). Sampling multiple colonies decreases sampling error. Basic Concepts: AST Modalities Demystifying the McFarland Standard 19 Most AST methods (manual and commercial) utilize a 0.5 McFarland standard Measurement of suspension turbidity 1-2x108 cfu/mL References created using barium sulfate Can be measured visually or with instrument Janet Hindler, ASM/CLSI Approximate Cell Density 1% H2SO4 (ml) 1% BaCl2(ml) McFarland Standard 1.5 x 10^8 9.95 0.05 0.5 3.0 x 10^8 9.9 0.1 1.0 6.0 x 10^8 9.8 0.2 2.0 9.0 x 10^8 9.7 0.3 3.0 12.0 x 10^8 9.6 0.4 4.0 Broth Microdilution (BMD) Panel inoculation 20 CLSI M07 ED 12 (2024) document Thaw completely to room temperature – Inoculate within 4 hours of thaw, do not refreeze Adjust McFarland so final well will have 2-8x105 cfu/mL Inoculate plates with 50 µL inoculum within 15 minutes – This will dilute the 2X concentrated antibiotic in the well to 1X in 100µL total volume Seal each panel (or stack of 4) in a plastic bag to prevent drying and incubate desired environment 0.5 McFarland = 1-2x108 cfu/mL Sample well contains 50µL antibiotic Inoculum will be 50µL 100µL total volume/well 1:100 dilution = 1-2x106 50µL bacteria at 1-2x106 cfu/mL + 50µL antibiotic 100µL at ~5x105 cfu/mL/well Broth Microdilution (BMD) Panel Incubation 21 CLSI M07 ED 12 (2024) document Incubation Conditions for Reference BMD 35°C +/- 2°C, ambient air, 16-20 hours Enterobacterales Other non-Enterobacterales 35°C +/- 2°C, ambient air, 20-24 hours No current breakpoints Acinetobacter sp.
Pseudomonas aeruginosa Stenotrophomonas maltophilia Burkholderia cepacia complex Haemophilus sp.
Streptococci 35°C +/- 2°C, ambient air, 16-20 hours Oxacillin/Vancomycin: 24 hours >35°C may not detect MRS Staphylococcus 35°C +/- 2°C, ambient air, 16-20 hours Vancomycin: 24 hours Enterococcus 35°C +/- 2°C, CO2, 16-20 hours N. meningitidis 36°C +/- 1°C, anaerobic, 46-48 hours Anaerobes Incubate the panels within 15 minutes of inoculation – Incubation conditions are determined by species – Some drug/bug combinations require prolonged incubation Thaw plates Inoculate plates Incubate Basic Concepts: AST Modalities Endpoints 22 Endpoint: a set of conditions outlining when data is collected and interpreted from an AST assay Endpoints can be influenced by multiple factors including: – Incubation time – Physical conditions (atmosphere, light) – Biological variation – Interpretive caveats – Artifacts A working understanding of the criteria for interpretation and acceptance of AST data is important Criteria for endpoint reading must be defined in SOPs Endpoint Susceptibility Test Assay Type MIC Agar Dilution Dilution Gradient MIC Broth Dilution MIC Gradient Diffusion Diffusion Gradient ZOI Disc Diffusion How are these values derived/interpreted?
Broth Microdilution (BMD) Minimal Inhibitory Concentrations 23 Minimal Inhibitory Concentration (MIC) – Lowest antibiotic concentration where no visible growth is detected Minimum Bactericidal Concentration (MBC) – lowest concentration of antibiotic which results in the killing of the organism Antibiotic (µg/mL) 4µg/mL 8µg/mL 2µg/mL Visible Growth No Growth 0 0.25 0.5 1 2 4 8 16 What is the MIC in this example?
What is the MBC in this example?
Additional testing needed 4µg/mL MBC: viable count The MIC (not MBC) is the endpoint of a dilution assay The MIC is the unit of measurement for clinical AST data Broth Microdilution (BMD) Minimal Inhibitory Concentrations – Conventions & Caveats 24 MIC: lowest concertation of drug that inhibits the growth of the organism as detected by the unaided eye The appearance of growth may differ based on the type of well used – Round bottom wells: growth appears as a button – Flat bottom wells: growth is more scattered The species of organism tested may impact the appearance of growth – Ps. aeruginosa and Acinetobacter sp. often exhibit hazy growth – Turbidity in the absence of a pellet should still be interpreted as growth – Increasing drug concentrations may also impact appearance BMD wells should only be read in the setting of sufficient growth in the control well Ps. aeruginosa A. baumannii MIC MIC Round Bottom Wells Flat Bottom Wells Adapted from Erika Matuschek, PhD – EUCAST Development Lab Isolate A: What’s the MIC?

small zones (i.e. vancomycin) next to those that produce larger zones (i.e. cephalosporins) – Discs must not be closer than 24mm center to center – For non-fastidious organisms: No more than: 12 discs/150mm plate or 6 discs/100mm plate – Fastidious organisms: species-dependent, see CLSI M07 Press discs gently to ensure contact with plate surface As diffusion happens almost instantaneously, discs cannot be relocated CLSI M02 ED 14 (2024) document / CLSI M100 ED35 (2025) document Incubation Conditions for DD 35°C +/- 2°C, ambient air, 16-18 hours Routine 35°C +/- 2°C, ambient air, 20-24 MTS) Routine testing Commercial Broth Microdilution Systems Labs often utilize manual AST methods as “backup methods” to address varied clinical scenarios – Testing drugs not on routine panel – Routine method has a “limitation” for a bug/drug combination – Organism is not FDA-cleared on the routine AST system – Testing fastidious bacteria that cannot be tested on the routine system Basic Concepts: Manual Methods Resistance Marker-Specific Testing and Interpretation Erythromycin Clindamycin Oxacillin Vancomycin A wound isolate of S. aureus was interpreted as follows: Erythromycin – R Clindamycin – S Oxacillin – S Vancomycin - S Are you OK with this interpretation? Why or why not?
Erythromycin – R Clindamycin – R Oxacillin – S Vancomycin - S Observed growth pattern is suggestive of inducible clindamycin resistance 56 Basic Concepts: Manual Methods Clindamycin Resistance Erythromycin Clindamycin Oxacillin Vancomycin Erythromycin (macrolide) and clindaymycin (lincosamide) inhibit protein synthesis 50S ribosomal target Resistance is mediated through methylation of the binding site by erm genes (MLSb) and efflux pumps Expression of erm can either be constitutive or inducible Constitutively expression: methylase always produced Inducible expression: methylase only produced in the presence of an inducer Constitutive erm expression is reliably detected by most routine modalities Detection of inducible erm expression requires specialized setup Inability to detect inducible resistance can result in treatment failures 57 Key point: Erythromycin is an efficient inducer of erm gene expression Constitutive expression results in resistance to both drugs Inducible expression results in resistance to erythromycin but susceptibility to clindamycin The “D”-Test Erythromycin and Clindamycin discs placed adjacent to ensure overlap in diffusible zones Staphylococci: MHA, 15-26mm apart, 35°C +/- 2°C, ambient, 16-18h Streptococci: MHA + 5% SB, 12 mm apart, 35°C +/- 2°C, CO2, 20-44h Organisms demonstrating inducible clindamycin resistance: S. aureus, Beta-hemolytic streptococci, S. pneumoniae Basic Concepts: Manual Methods Inducible Clindamycin Resistance Induced Clindamycin resistance by Erythromycin exposure CLSI M100 Ed 35 (2025) – Table 3J 58 Basic Concepts: Manual Methods Inducible Clindamycin Resistance – the D-test CLSI M100 Ed 35 (2025) – Table 3J Erm S Clinda S erm gene not present Erm R Clinda R Suggestive of constitutive expression of erm Erm R Clinda S erm gene not present Erythromycin resistance mediated by alternative mechanism (efflux) Erm R Clinda R Suggestive of inducible expression of erm 59 Basic Concepts: AST Modalities Integration of manual and automated methods 60 Manual and automated methods can be utilized in tandem depending on laboratory needs Automated methods: commercial systems with automated readers/panels streamline workflow, standardize interpretations, and decrease turnaround time Manual methods: labor-intensive, helpful for low volume testing of off-panel targets Using together allows testing of compounds that are only supported by specific modalities, used for special indications, or are associated with technical complexity Ps. aeruginosa Routine AST Release additional on-panel drugs Ceftazidime/Avibactam Ceftolozane/Tazobactam Cefiderocol Imi/Rel Manual KB/Etest Manual CBDE (colistin only) Basic Concepts: AST Modalities Integration of manual and automated methods 61 40 y/o male with a history of cardiomyopathy requiring an LVAD presents complaining of pain at the exit site. Upon exam, the driveline exit site was tender and rust colored drainage was noted. Blood cultures and cultures of the exit site were sent to the microbiology laboratory, which subsequently grew Staphylococcus aureus with the following susceptibility profile: Staphylococcus aureus, Methicillin-susceptible S Oxacillin S Daptomycin S Linezolid R Tetracycline S Trimethoprim/Sulfamethoxazole S Clindamycin I Vancomycin What is unusual concerning this susceptibility profile?
The laboratory utilizes an automated BMD system. What can be done to investigate this abnormal result?
Manual inspection of the BMD wells Adjunctive testing using a manual MIC “backup” method Result: Vancomycin-intermediate S. aureus R I S Vancomycin/S. aureus ≥16 8 ≤4 CLSI 2025 Basic Concepts: AST Modalities Overview of Automated Methods 62 Advantages/Disadvantages of Automation Reduced labor Increased standardization in panel reads Interface capability with LIS Rapid resulting Covers most non-fastidious organisms Advantages High initial financial cost Limited flexibility of panel composition Inability to test fastidious organisms Inability to detect contamination (purity plates) Need for “back-up” system Shortened incubation may miss some resistance mechanisms Disadvantages Features of Automated Systems “Hands-off” incubation/reading functions Robotics – Automated movement of panels/cuvettes – Addition of reagents as necessary Increased optical sensitivity for growth detection Interpretation of endpoints in accordance with standardized databases Computer synthesis of data – Store/retrieve results – Merge ID/AST data on a single report Basic Concepts: AST Modalities Overview of Automated Methods 63 **Important: This is not a recommendation or endorsement for the use of one platform over another, rather a high-level survey and discussion of how routine laboratory instrumentation interprets data** Manual AST methods are labor-intensive Many laboratories utilized automated methods in order to streamline workflow, standardize interpretations, and decrease turnaround time A working understanding of how instruments interpret growth patterns is important resistance mechanisms A well for every MIC reported is not necessary https://www.biomerieux-microbio.com/how-does-vitek-2-generate-mic-values/ Basic Concepts: AST Modalities Vitek 2 66 Broth-based microdilution assay Samples normalized to 0.5 McFarland Inoculated into broth which is dispensed into 96-well plates dosed with various antibiotic dilations Growth can be detected by manual inspection, colorimetric reaction, or fluorescence Fluorescent technology monitors activity of bacterial surface enzymes 1.
Enzyme substrates conjugated with fluorophore compound 2.
Conjugation involves bonding to prevent fluorescence (quinch) 3.
Enzymatic activity (growth) detected when bond is cleaved, releasing the fluorophore resulting in fluorescence 4.
Level of detected fluorescence is proportional to enzyme activity which is proportional to growth https://www.thermofisher.com/us/en/home/clinical/clinical-microbiology/antimicrobial-susceptibility-testing/features.html Basic Concepts: AST Modalities Sensititre 67 Broth-based microdilution assay Test panels similar to 96 well plates -demarcated by compound Culture growth standardized by dilution or Prompt Wells monitored continuously between 18-24 hours Colorimetric/fluorometric optical systems detect color and fluorescent changes, as well as alterations in well turbidity to determine positivity Basic Concepts: AST Modalities Microscan Walkaway 68 Basic Concepts: Automated Methods Minimal Inhibitory Concentrations 69 In automated platforms, manufacturers may integrate MIC testing in various footprints – Panels are designed to test a broad range or group of bacteria (i.e. Gram-negative) Panels are configured with different dilution ranges and drugs to meet laboratory needs Not all drugs will receive a full dilution series 0.25 0.5 1 2 Complete Range S R I Partial Range/Extrapolated MIC 0.5 2 4 R S Breakpoint Testing 2 4 R S It is important to understand the range and limitations of your selected panels Basic Concepts: Automated Methods Resistance Marker-Specific Testing and Interpretation The following is a raw susceptibility profile obtained from an E. coli isolate from urine.
Based on the following profile, what would we consider reporting to the chart?
Notable Features 1. Broad resistance to 3rd and 4th generation cephalosporins (CAX,CAZ,CPE) 2. Retained susceptibility to cefoxitin (cefamycin, CFX) 3. >2 doubling dilution difference with the addition of clavulanate (CAZ,CFT/CA) Extended spectrum beta-lactamase producer 70 Basic Concepts: Automated Methods Extended Spectrum Beta-Lactamases (ESBLs) Many commercial AST systems will incorporate wells for ESBL screening Only able to be reported for E. coli, K. oxytoca, K. pneumoniae, and P. mirabilis ESBLs are found on plasmids, transposons, and in the chromosome (i.e. Aeromonas) Hundreds of types and variants: CTX-M, TEM, SHV variants – one reason there is no “pan ESBL-PCR” Common Resistance Pattern Hydrolyze extended-spectrum cephalosporins (3rd-4th generation); susceptibility to cephamycins Inhibition by: clavulanate, sulbactam, tazobactam, avibactam, relebactam, and vaborbactam Variable hydrolysis of different cephalosporins CTX-M generally hydrolyzes cefotaxime/ceftriaxone better than ceftazidime Convention is to test 2+ 3rd generation cephalosporins for routine ESBL testing 71 Basic Concepts: Automated Methods Resistance Marker-specific Testing and Interpretation 72 You are working in a laboratory which primarily utilizes DD testing and generate the following raw susceptibility profile for a K. pneumoniae isolate. Could this be an ESBL?
Ciprofloxacin R Amx/clav S Ampicillin R Levofloxacin R Pip/taz I Cefazolin R Gentamicin S Aztreonam R Cefepime R Amikacin S Cefotaxime R Tobramycin R Ertapenem S Cefoxitin S Tetracycline R Imipenem S Ceftazidime S Trimeth/sul R Meropenem S Ceftriaxone R How might you confirm this using manual methods?
Yes!

ESBL screen inconclusive (should you attempt a manual method?) 2. Resistance to cefoxitin (cefamycin, CFX), susceptibility to cefepime 3. AmpC beta-lactamase producer 74 AmpC is a cephalosporinase chromosomally carried by some Enterobacterales Compared to ESBLs, AmpC is: – Not inhibited by clavulanic acid, sulbactam, or tazobactam – Do not hydrolyze 4th generation cephalosporins – Hydrolyze cephamycins (cefoxitin/cefotetan) Basic Concepts: Automated Methods Resistance Marker-Specific Testing and Interpretation Expression level is important - just because the gene is present doesn’t mean it will impact resistance!!

Consider this example for Trimethoprim/Sulfamethoxazole (SXT).
Thymidine-free growth (TFG) well. Some strains of bacteria require external thymidine for growth (auxotrophy) SXT wells are intestinally devoid of thymidine to prevent inhibition of the antibiotic Thymidine auxatrophs may exhibit false susceptibility to SXT Is the lack of growth in the SXT well due to the activity of SXT, or the lack of thymidine?
Solution: If an organism does not grow in the TFG, a result should not be reported for SXT Basic Concepts: Automated Methods Drug-specific Testing and Interpretation 81 62 F with diffuse large B cell lymphoma status post EPOCH 1 year prior and treatment-related AML 6 months prior was admitted for a matched unrelated donor (MUD) allogenic stem cell transplant. During conditioning, she became febrile (Tmax 102), hypotensive and tachycardic. Blood cultures became positive after 12 hours with GPCs identified as Enterococcus gallinarum. Why is gentamicin released when Enterococci are intrinsically resistant to aminoglycosides?

Enterococci have intrinsic low-level aminoglycoside resistance Acquired resistance to high-level aminoglycoside resistance (HLAR); aminoglycoside-modifying enzymes If HLAR resistance is detected, synergistic approaches are not actionable Testing for HLAR; Gentamicin and Streptomycin; dedicated wells on panel To be released on blood sources only Basic Concepts: Automated Methods Drug-specific Testing and Interpretation 30S 30S Gentamicin Gentamicin Monotherapy Gentamicin Synergy Beta-lactam or vancomycin induced gaps in cell wall Inhibition of protein synthesis 82 Basic Concepts: Quality Control QC isolate recommendations and guidance 83 Typically, routine AST methods are tested weekly (IQCP) with defined QC strains – Ensures antibiotic potency – Ensures liquid media supports growth Less frequently utilized antibiotics may be QC-tested with each reagent lot and at each time of use QC organisms defined by the CLSI (manual methods) or the commercial package insert (automated methods) QC Bug/Drug combinations must fall within an established range to report results CLSI M100 Document: Tables 4A-1 / 5A-1 CLSI QC recommendations for cefepime DD and MIC methods Basic Concepts: Quality Control Beta-lactam/Beta-lactamase inhibitor combination agents 84 CLSI M100 Document: Tables 4A-2 Routine QC recommended strains Either strain is appropriate for Routine QC (green) • Drug-specific QC check Test this strain against this drug to ensure the integrity of the strain (orange) • Strain-specific QC check • Performed once when strain first recovered from lyophilization Basic Concepts: Quality Control QC Stock Maintenance – General Guidance 85 Important exceptions New F1 subcultures prepared every 2 weeks for the following: E. faecalis ATCC 51299 - 99 The result for the clinical team is the interpretation (or outcome) of the susceptibility test What are the interpretive categories and how are they defined? Interpretive Categories Recognized by US Regulatory Authorities FDA CLSI Susceptible Susceptible Susceptible-dose dependent (New 8/10/2023) Susceptible-dose dependent Intermediate Intermediate Resistant Resistant Nonsusceptible Basic Concepts: Breakpoints Interpretive Criteria 100 Basic Categorical Interpretation Susceptible Intermediate Resistant Isolates with an MIC at or below or ZOI at or above are inhibited by the usually achievable concentrations of antibiotic recommended to treat the site of infection is used, likely resulting in clinical efficacy Isolates with an MIC/ZOI diameters within the intermediate rate that approach usually attainable blood/tissue levels and/or for which response rate may be lower than for susceptible isolates Isolates with an MIC at or above or ZOI at or below are not inhibited by the usually achievable concentrations of antibiotic with normal dosing schedules and/or demonstrate MICs/ZOIs which fall within a range in which specific microbial resistance mechanisms are likely, and clinical efficacy of the agent against the isolate has not been reliably demonstrated.
CLSI M100, 35th Ed. (2025) Basic Concepts: Breakpoints Interpretive Criteria 101 Basic Categorical Interpretation Susceptible-Dose Dependent Non-susceptible Susceptibility of an isolate depends on the dosage regimen that is used in the patient. To achieve levels that are likely to be clinically effective it is necessary to use a dosage regimen (ie, higher doses, more frequent doses, extended infusion) that results in higher drug exposure than that achieved with the dose that was used to establish the susceptible breakpoint.
used for isolates for which only a susceptible breakpoint is designated because of the absence or rare occurrence of resistant strains. Isolates for which the antimicrobial agent MICs are above or the zone diameters are below the value indicated for the susceptible breakpoint should be reported as non-susceptible. Note: NS does NOT necessarily mean a resistance mechanism is present (does NOT mean I or R) Basic Concepts: Breakpoints Interpretive Criteria 102 Drug information Categorical Interpretation Dosing information for for SDD Category 103 Basic Concepts: Bug/Drug Combos Utilizing SDD (2025) Resistant Intermediate Susceptible Dose-Dependent Susceptible Organism ≥8 2-4 ≤1 S. aureus Ceftaroline CLSI ≥4 2 ≤1 Ceftaroline FDA ≥16 4-8 ≤2 Enterobacterales Cefepime CLSI Cefepime FDA ≥32/4 16/4 ≤8/4 Enterobacterales Pip/Tazo CLSI ≥32/4 16/4 ≤8/4 Pip/Tazo FDA ≥8 ≤4 E. faecium Daptomycin CLSI Daptomycin FDA 104 Basic Concepts: Non-susceptible interpretation 105 The FDA has regulatory authority over medical devices marketed in the US Susceptibility testing instrumentation are considered Class II Medical Devices The Agency also publishes and maintains a set of antibacterial and antifungal interpretive breakpoints for susceptibility testing These breakpoints are the interpretive criteria utilized when device manufacturers submit for clearance So where do I find these values?

CLSI M100, 35th Ed. (2025) 126 Sometimes, despite our best efforts, abnormal or problematic AST results arise These can include: 1.
Atypical resistance patterns associated with different organisms 2.
Erroneous or accidental reporting of AST data Critical evaluation of susceptibility data reported by both your and outside institutions is an important skill to develop This develops the following skillsets: 1.
Familiarity with susceptibility phenotypes different organism groups 2.
Knowledge of important caveats concerning interpretation 3.
Troubleshooting skills to accurately address problems and errors Basic Concepts: Evaluation of AST Data Addressing Abnormal or Problematic Values 127 27 y/o F who recently underwent breast reconstructive surgery at an outside institution as part of management of an invasive malignancy presents with drainage from one of her incision sites. Culture was positive for Enterobacter cloacae complex, and the following susceptibility profile was released: Question to consider: Is there anything abnormal about this AST report?
How might this report be corrected/amended?

It is important to be aware of these limitations and to provide appropriate clinical context to providers Hints/Approaches when dealing with potential limitations: 1.

# Determination of MIC Quality Control Parameters for Exebacase, a Novel Lysin with Antistaphylococcal Activity

### Maria M Traczewski

### Jane E Ambler

### Raymond Schuch

**Citation** Traczewski MM, Ambler JE, Schuch R. 2021. Determination of MIC quality control parameters for exebacase, a novel lysin with antistaphylococcal activity. J Clin Microbiol 59:e03117-20. <https://doi.org/10.1128/JCM.03117-20>.

Corresponding author.

#### Roles

Received 2020 Dec 10; Revision requested 2021 Jan 7; Accepted 2021 Apr 16; Prepublished 2021 Apr 28; Collection date 2021 Jul.

This is an open-access article distributed under the terms of the [Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International license](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/).

## ABSTRACT

Exebacase (CF-301), a novel, antistaphylococcal lysin (cell wall hydrolase) is the first agent of this class to enter late-stage clinical development (phase 3, [NCT04160468](https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT04160468)) for the treatment of *Staphylococcus aureus* bacteremia, including right-sided endocarditis. A multilaboratory Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute (CLSI) M23-defined tier 2 quality control (QC) study was conducted to establish exebacase QC ranges for a new reference broth microdilution method. *S. aureus* ATCC 29213 and *Enterococcus faecalis* ATCC 29212 were selected as reference QC strains. Broth microdilution MIC QC ranges for exebacase spanned 4 log2 dilutions and contained 99.2% of the MIC results generated for the two reference strains. The QC ranges for exebacase were defined as 0.25 to 2 μg/ml and 8 to 64 μg/ml against *S. aureus* ATCC 29213 and *E. faecalis* ATCC 29212, respectively, and were approved by the CLSI Subcommittee on Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing. These QC ranges established for use with the reference broth microdilution method developed for exebacase susceptibility testing will ensure the test performance and accuracy of results generated during clinical development.

**KEYWORDS:** exebacase, CF-301, lysin, QC range, lysin

## INTRODUCTION

Exebacase is a first-in-class protein therapeutic, organized into two distinct functional domains, a C-terminal domain, which binds with high affinity to conserved peptidoglycan ligands or cell wall-associated glycopolymers and an N-terminal catalytic domain that enzymatically cleaves peptidoglycan bonds within the cell wall. Exebacase elicits direct lytic activity against specific target bacteria and thereby demonstrates the following microbiological attributes: rapid, specific bactericidal activity; the ability to eradicate staphylococcal biofilms; synergy with antistaphylococcal antibiotics; a low propensity to develop resistance; no cross-resistance with antibiotics; and an extended postantibiotic effect ([1](#B1)[–](#B2)[4](#B4)).

In preliminary *in vitro* susceptibility studies, pronounced trailing was observed for all *Staphylococcus aureus* isolates tested in triplicate, including the quality control (QC) strains, using cation-adjusted Mueller-Hinton broth (CAMHB) ([5](#B5)). Trailing frequently obscured MIC endpoints and resulted in poor MIC reproducibility. Additionally, a loss of activity was reported when frozen form MIC panels (prepared with CAMHB) were used for MIC testing, yielding higher MIC results. For these reasons, modifications to the standard broth microdilution reference method were systematically investigated following protocols outlined in CLSI M23-A3 in order to eliminate the trailing effect and develop a reference broth microdilution method for testing exebacase susceptibility against clinical isolates ([5](#B5), [6](#B6)). A modified broth microdilution method using CAMHB supplemented with 25% horse serum and 0.5 mM dl-dithiothreitol was developed at ContraFect for this first-in-class protein-based therapeutic and approved by the CLSI Subcommittee on Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing in 2017 ([7](#B7)). The development of this modified broth microdilution method and the conduct of initial M23-style QC (tier 1) studies to identify preliminary QC ranges are described elsewhere ([5](#B5)).

Early establishment of expected QC ranges is necessary for the effective evaluation of antibacterial test performance and to thereby ensure that accurate, reproducible, and robust MIC data are generated in clinical development. Exebacase susceptibility testing of clinical trial isolates was performed in the recently completed phase 2 (ClinicalTrials.gov identifier [NCT03163446](https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT03163446)) superiority design study that established proof of concept for exebacase as a potential to treatment for methicillin-resistant *S. aureus* (MRSA) bloodstream infections ([16](#B16)). As for all new antibacterial agents, it is essential to monitor QC data continually during clinical trials and to note deviations from the modal MIC as well as potential trending of higher or lower MIC values, in particular when an unfamiliar modified method is to be employed by a central laboratory and testing large numbers of clinical isolates collected as part of a large antibacterial surveillance program. Traczewski et al. reported exebacase *in vitro* activity using the modified broth microdilution method (MIC values of ≤1 μg/ml) against 535 *S. aureus* (MRSA and methicillin-susceptible *S. aureus* [MSSA]) isolates collected from medical centers in the United States, Europe, and South America ([8](#B8)). The current report describes the design and results of a multilaboratory broth microdilution MIC QC study conducted to evaluate expected exebacase MIC QC ranges against relevant ATCC strains recommended by CLSI.

(Data from this study were presented at the ASM Microbe 2017, New Orleans, LA, 1 to 5 June 2017 [[9](#B9)].)

## MATERIALS AND METHODS

### Participating laboratories.

Eight independent laboratories (laboratory directors shown in parentheses) participated in this study by performing broth microdilution MIC testing following CLSI guidelines (M07-A10 [[10](#B10)] and M100 [[11](#B11)]): Clinical Microbiology Institute, Wilsonville, OR (Maria M. Traczewski); the UCLA Medical Center, Microbiology Laboratory, Los Angeles, CA (Janet Hindler); the University of Rochester Medical Center, Microbiology Laboratory, Rochester, NY (Dwight J. Hardy); International Health Management Associates Inc., Schaumburg, IL (Dana Dressel); Thermo Fisher Scientific, Oakwood Village, OH (Cindy Knapp); Laboratory Specialists Inc., Westlake, OH (Laura M. Koeth); Tufts New England Medical Center, Boston, MA (Laura McDermott); Micromyx, Kalamazoo, MI (Chris Pillar). The Clinical Microbiology Institute coordinated the distribution of necessary laboratory supplies to the other seven laboratories and conducted the analysis of the MIC results.

### Antibacterial agents.

Exebacase (molecular weight, 25,928 Da) was provided by ContraFect Corp., Yonkers, NY, as frozen solution (lot NB0020-11-13) at a concentration of 10 mg/ml in a buffer (pH 7) containing 20 mM l-histidine and 5% d-sorbitol. Vancomycin (lot SLBM3283V) purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, was used as an internal control test agent. A vancomycin stock solution was prepared by dissolving in water and diluted to a final concentration of 1,280 μg/ml.

### Bacterial isolates and culture media.

The two CLSI-recommended QC strains selected for testing were *S. aureus* ATCC 29213 and *Enterococcus faecalis* ATCC 29212. Exebacase has specific activity against *Staphylococcus* species and group A, B, and G *Streptococcus* species ([12](#B12)). Since exebacase has no clinically relevant activity against *E. faecalis*, QC ranges were established against *E. faecalis* ATCC 29212 in order to evaluate the performance of the test method should isolates with exebacase MICs of >2 μg/ml be encountered.

The Clinical Microbiology Institute prepared three lots of broth microdilution panels to test exebacase susceptibility. Exebacase stock solution was diluted (0.12 to 128 μg/ml) using three different commercial lots of Mueller-Hinton broth (MHB) manufactured by three different companies: lot 1 was BBL Mueller-Hinton II (cation-adjusted) broth (catalog no. 212322, lot 5257869; Becton Dickinson, Franklin Lakes, NJ) and required no adjustment for cations, whereas lot 2 from Difco Mueller-Hinton Broth (catalog no. 275730, lot 4045151; Becton Dickinson) and lot 3 from Oxoid Mueller-Hinton Broth (catalog no. CM0405B, lot 1743805; Thermo Fisher Scientific, Bedford, MA) required supplementation with divalent cations Mg2+ and Ca2+ after sterilization as described in CLSI standard M07-A10 ([10](#B10)). All three lots were supplemented with 25% horse serum (Sigma-Aldrich) and 0.5 mM dl-dithiothreitol (Sigma-Aldrich); following the aseptic addition of these supplements, the pH ranged from 7.2 to 7.4. Vancomycin solution was diluted in lot 1 BBL Mueller-Hinton II (cation-adjusted) broth without supplementation (horse serum or dl-dithiothreitol). All freshly prepared MIC panels were placed in plastic bags and immediately frozen at −70°C, shipped to each of the participating laboratories frozen, and stored at −70°C until use.

### Broth microdilution susceptibility testing.

Quality control strains *S. aureus* ATCC 29213 and *E. faecalis* ATCC 29212 were subcultured from freezer stock 2 days prior to testing. Each laboratory tested 10 replicates of each QC strain, and the number of replicates tested per day was left to the discretion of each of the participating laboratories; however, in accordance with CLSI M23-A3 guidelines, all testing was performed within a minimum of 3 days. Each laboratory generated a total of 30 MIC results for each of the two QC strains tested (an MIC result for each of the 10 replicates, tested in three different lots of CAMHB). Inocula were prepared in 0.85% saline to match a McFarland 0.5 turbidity standard using colonies taken from a subculture on sheep blood agar plate incubated overnight for 18 to 24 h prior to each test day. Each replicate was tested using a unique 0.5 McFarland preparation. Each inoculum preparation was further diluted with saline to obtain a final inoculum of approximately 5 × 105 CFU/ml in the test panel. For *S. aureus* ATCC 29215, the modal inoculum colony count was 4.8 × 105 CFU/ml, and for *E. faecalis*, it was 4.4 × 105 CFU/ml. The inoculated MIC panels were incubated according to CLSI M07 ([10](#B10)). Each laboratory performed colony counts of the inoculum density on drug-free agar, on each day of testing. Appropriate assay performance was verified by concurrent MIC testing of vancomycin as an internal control agent. Laboratories were instructed to perform repeat tests if any of the vancomycin results were out of range on the day of testing.

### Analysis of QC ranges.

All MIC and colony count data reported were submitted to the Clinical Microbiology Institute for analysis. The study results were analyzed using criteria outlined in M23-A3 ([6](#B6)), and analyses of MIC results were done using the RangeFinder statistical program and methods described by Turnidge and Bordash ([13](#B13)).

## RESULTS

The exebacase broth microdilution MIC results (in micrograms per milliliter) against *S. aureus* ATCC 29213 and *E. faecalis* ATCC 29212 for each participating laboratory and each of the three commercial MHB lots tested are presented in [Table 1](#T1).

### TABLE 1.

Medium lot and inter- and intralaboratory comparisons of exebacase broth microdilution MICs obtained against *S. aureus* ATCC 29213 and *E. faecalis* ATCC 29212

Geometric mean of observed values.

For *S. aureus* ATCC 29213, the overall distribution of MIC results was 5 log2 dilution steps (0.25 to 4 μg/ml), 44.6% of results were at the mode (0.5 μg/ml), and 94.2% of results were within 1 doubling dilution of the mode. The MIC range for each laboratory was 2 to 4 log2 dilution steps. The results were analyzed by individual medium lots: for commercial MHB lots 2 and 3, which required supplemental cation adjustments (M07), the distribution of MIC values was 3 and 4 log2 dilutions, with 51.3% and 76.2% of results at the mode (1 μg/ml) and 100% and 97.2% of results within 1 doubling dilution of the mode, respectively. However, MHB lot 1, which required no cation adjustment, generated an MIC distribution of 2 log2 dilutions, with 78.7% of results at the mode (0.5 μg/ml) and 100% within 1 doubling dilution of the mode.

For *E. faecalis* ATCC 29212, the overall distribution of MIC results was 5 log2 dilution steps (8 to 128 μg/ml), 55.4% of results were at the mode (16 μg/ml) and 94.6% of results were within 1 doubling dilution of the mode. The MIC range generated by each laboratory was 2 to 4 log2 dilution steps. When analyzed by individual medium lots, with commercial MHB lots 2 and 3, the distributions of MIC values were 4 and 5 log2 dilutions, with 72.5% and 58.7% of results at the mode (32 and 16 μg/ml, respectively) and 100% and 97.2% of results within 1 doubling dilution of the respective modes for each lot. Whereas less variability in MIC results was reported with MHB lot 1 which generated an MIC distribution of 3 log2 dilutions, with 91.2% of results at the mode (16 μg/ml) and 100% within 1 doubling dilution of the mode. Overall, there was a trend toward higher MIC results for both QC strains with the use of medium lots 2 and 3. These media generated four out-of-range MIC results reported by three different laboratories.

All vancomycin MIC results against the QC strains tested (not shown) were within published QC ranges (100% overall), and thereby provided a valid internal control for the study.

QC ranges for exebacase were calculated using both CLSI M23-A3 criteria and RangeFinder methods, which resulted in the recommendation of a 4-log2-unit QC range for both ATCC strains. Both *S. aureus* ATCC 29213 and *E. faecalis* ATCC 29212 QC ranges had a bimodal distribution of values, and no statistical differences as determined by the RangeFinder statistical program analysis were observed. The ranges recommended to the CLSI Subcommittee for Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing were 0.25 to 1 μg/ml for *S. aureus* ATCC 29213 and 8 to 64 μg/ml for *E. faecalis* ATCC 29212. Importantly, none of the eight participating labs noted a trailing effect when testing the two QC strains.

## DISCUSSION

The results of this multilaboratory study demonstrated that the new exebacase broth microdilution MIC method developed by ContraFect using CAMHB supplemented with 25% horse serum and 0.5 mM DTT produced acceptable intralaboratory and interlaboratory reproducibility of MIC results with replicate testing of CLSI QC reference strains *S. aureus* ATCC 29213 and *E. faecalis* ATCC 29212. The results confirmed that MIC endpoints determined for both of the two ATCC strains tested were easy to read by different laboratory personnel and generated reproducible results. The studies described here met CLSI document M23-A3 ([6](#B6)) requirements for tier 2 QC studies to establish MIC ranges for this novel protein antibacterial agent.

The MIC data generated by the eight participating laboratories produced a four-dilution QC range for each of the QC strains tested, *S. aureus* ATCC 29213 and *E. faecalis* ATCC 29212 ([Fig. 1](#F1)). [Table 2](#T2) summarizes the MIC QC ranges evaluated in this study and approved by the CLSI AST Subcommittee to be used with the exebacase broth microdilution method. For both of the ATCC strains evaluated, 99.2% of MIC values reported by the eight laboratories were within range. Two different laboratories (lab 2 and 3) each reported a single out-of-range exebacase MIC result of 4 μg/ml against *S. aureus* ATCC 29213. Various factors such as growth medium, pH, divalent cation concentration, and age of medium are known to influence broth microdilution susceptibility test results ([14](#B14)). Out-of-range QC results may be directly attributed to the medium used for testing. In this study, the commercial source of CAMHB influenced the extent of MIC variability, with medium lots that require additional cation supplementation having 3 and 4 log2 dilutions against *S. aureus* ATCC 29213 and 4 and 5 log2 dilutions against *E. faecalis* ATCC 29212. This contrasts with medium lot 1, where the MIC range was 2 and 3 log2 dilutions against ATCC 29213 and ATCC 29212, respectively ([Table 1](#T1)).

### FIG 1.

[![FIG 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/3b20/8218745/da2e7c608ca6/jcm.03117-20-f0001.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=8218745_jcm.03117-20-f0001.jpg)

![FIG 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/3b20/8218745/da2e7c608ca6/jcm.03117-20-f0001.jpg)

Distribution of exebacase broth microdilution MIC test results against ATCC QC strains *S. aureus* ATCC 29213 (A) and *E. faecalis* ATCC 29212 (B). The medium lots are indicated as follows: blue, medium lot 1; white, medium lot 2; red, medium lot 3.

### TABLE 2.

Summary of CLSI-approved broth microdilution MIC QC ranges for exebacase

| Strain | MIC range (μg/ml) | Occurrences (%) in range |
| --- | --- | --- |
| *S. aureus* ATCC 29213 | 0.25–2 | 99.2 |
| *E. faecalis* ATCC 29212 | 8–64 | 99.2 |

In conclusion, the results and analyses from this study led to the approval of exebacase MIC QC ranges summarized in [Table 2](#T2) by the CLSI Subcommittee for Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing and subsequent publication in CLSI standard M100 ([15](#B15)). Exebacase is the first protein-based antibacterial biologic to be reviewed by a standards development organization and to establish QC ranges for a broth microdilution method. The approved QC ranges will allow continued monitoring of QC data to ensure that exebacase MIC results generated by a variety of laboratories during clinical development can reliably assess the accuracy and reproducibility of MIC results and test performance when testing clinical isolates. A superiority design phase 3 study to assess the efficacy and safety of exebacase in patients with *S. aureus* bacteremia, including right-sided endocarditis is ongoing (<https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/results?cond=&term=NCT04160468&cntry=&state=&city=&dist=>). Continued monitoring of QC data during testing of clinical trial and surveillance isolates will allow further assessment of expected exebacase QC ranges to establish whether the current QC ranges published in CLSI document M100 should remain unchanged or require modification. As susceptibility testing of additional *Staphylococcus* species is performed, reading of MIC endpoints, MIC reproducibility, and interlaboratory variation will be further assessed and consideration given to recommendations for specific instructions for reading and interpreting MIC tests (e.g., 80% or 100% inhibition), including photographs to assist the reader with these instructions.

## ACKNOWLEDGMENTS

We thank the laboratory directors at the participating laboratories and their staff for conducting the study according to the protocol.

This study was sponsored by a research grant from ContraFect Corp. M.M.T. is an employee of the Clinical Microbiology Institute, which received funding to study exebacase in the development of these studies. J.E.A. and R.S. are employees of ContraFect Corp.

All authors provided analysis input and read and approved the final article.

M100 Performance Standards for Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing This document includes updated tables for the Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute antimicrobial susceptibility testing standards M02, M07, and M11.
A CLSI supplement for global application.
30th Edition CLSI eCLIPSE - Dewanand Mahto - BD - 01/29/2020. Unauthorized duplication or network sharing is not allowed.
Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute Setting the standard for quality in medical laboratory testing around the world.
The Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute (CLSI) is a not-for-profit membership organization that brings together the varied perspectives and expertise of the worldwide laboratory community for the advancement of a common cause: to foster excellence in laboratory medicine by developing and implementing medical laboratory standards and guidelines that help laboratories fulfill their responsibilities with efficiency, effectiveness, and global applicability. Consensus Process Consensus—the substantial agreement by materially affected, competent, and interested parties—is core to the development of all CLSI documents. It does not always connote unanimous agreement but does mean that the participants in the development of a consensus document have considered and resolved all relevant objections and accept the resulting agreement. Commenting on Documents CLSI documents undergo periodic evaluation and modification to keep pace with advances in technologies, procedures, methods, and protocols affecting the laboratory or health care.
CLSI’s consensus process depends on experts who volunteer to serve as contributing authors and/or as participants in the reviewing and commenting process. At the end of each comment period, the committee that developed the document is obligated to review all comments, respond in writing to all substantive comments, and revise the draft document as appropriate. Comments on published CLSI documents are equally essential and may be submitted by anyone, at any time, on any document. All comments are managed according to the consensus process by a committee of experts. Appeal Process When it is believed that an objection has not been adequately considered and responded to, the process for appeal, documented in the CLSI Standards Development Policies and Processes, is followed.
All comments and responses submitted on draft and published documents are retained on file at CLSI and are available upon request. Get Involved—Volunteer!
Do you use CLSI documents in your workplace? Do you see room for improvement? Would you like to get involved in the revision process? Or maybe you see a need to develop a new document for an emerging technology? CLSI wants to hear from you. We are always looking for volunteers. By donating your time and talents to improve the standards that affect your own work, you will play an active role in improving public health across the globe.
For additional information on committee participation or to submit comments, contact CLSI.

Tamma, MD, MHS Abstract The data in the tables are valid only if the methodologies in CLSI documents M02,1 M07,2 and M113 are followed. These standards contain information about disk diffusion (M021) and dilution (M072 and M113) test procedures for aerobic and anaerobic bacteria. Clinicians depend heavily on information from the microbiology laboratory for treating their seriously ill patients. The clinical importance of antimicrobial susceptibility test results demands that these tests be performed under optimal conditions and that laboratories have the capability to provide results for the newest antimicrobial agents. The tables presented in M100 represent the most current information for drug selection, interpretation, and quality control using the procedures standardized in M02,1 M07,2 and M11.3 Users should replace previously published tables with these new tables. Changes in the tables since the previous edition appear in boldface type. Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute (CLSI). Performance Standards for Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing. 29th ed. M100, 30th ed. xiv Overview of Changes M100, 30th ed. replaces the previous edition of the supplement, M100, 29th ed., published in 2019. The major changes in M100, 30th ed., are listed below. Other minor or editorial changes were made to the general formatting and to some of the table footnotes and comments. Changes to the tables since the previous edition appear in boldface type. The following are additions or changes unless otherwise noted as a “deletion.” Users of M100, 30th ed. should note recent and new formatting changes to Tables 2, including:  Intermediate ranges denoted with a “^” for the applicable antimicrobial agents in the drug groups in Tables 2 are based on the known ability of these agents to concentrate in the urine; some agents may also have the potential to concentrate at other anatomical sites (ie, epithelial lining). M100 is updated and reviewed annually as new data and new agents become available. Use of outdated documents is strongly discouraged. Section/Table Change(s) General Throughout the document Replaced:  “Coagulase-negative staphylococci (CoNS)” with “other Staphylococcus spp.”  The term “infection control” with “infection prevention” Clarified:  Methicillin and oxacillin terminology for Staphylococcus spp. Updated:  Genera formerly included in the family Enterobacteriaceae reorganized to an order (Enterobacterales) containing seven families: Budviciaceae, Enterobacteriaceae, Erwiniaceae, Hafniaceae, Morganellaceae, Pectobacteriaceae, Yersiniaceae4  Nomenclature for Salmonella Typhi to Salmonella enterica ser. Typhi  Nomenclature for Salmonella Paratyphi to Salmonella enterica ser. Paratyphi Overview of Changes CLSI eCLIPSE - Dewanand Mahto - BD - 01/29/2020. xxvii M100, 30th ed. Summary of CLSI Processes for Establishing Breakpoints and Quality Control Ranges The Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute (CLSI) is an international, voluntary, not-for-profit, interdisciplinary, standards-developing, and educational organization accredited by the American National Standards Institute that develops and promotes the use of consensus-developed standards and guidelines within the health care community. These consensus standards and guidelines are developed in an open and consensus-seeking forum to cover critical areas of diagnostic testing and patient health care. CLSI is open to anyone or any organization that has an interest in diagnostic testing and patient care. Information about CLSI can be found at www.clsi.org. The CLSI Subcommittee on Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing reviews data from a variety of sources and studies (eg, in vitro, pharmacokinetics-pharmacodynamics, and clinical studies) to establish antimicrobial susceptibility test methods, breakpoints, and QC parameters. The details of the data necessary to establish breakpoints, QC parameters, and how the data are presented for evaluation are described in CLSI document M23.5 Over time, a microorganism’s susceptibility to an antimicrobial agent may decrease, resulting in a lack of clinical efficacy and/or safety. In addition, microbiological methods and QC parameters may be refined to ensure more accurate and better performance of susceptibility test methods. Because of these types of changes, CLSI continually monitors and updates information in its documents. Although CLSI standards and guidelines are developed using the most current information available at the time, the field of science and medicine is always changing; therefore, standards and guidelines should be used in conjunction with clinical judgment, current knowledge, and clinically relevant laboratory test results to guide patient treatment. Additional information, updates, and changes in this document are found in the meeting summary minutes of the Subcommittee on Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing at https://clsi.org/meetings/ast-file-resources/. xxviii M100, 30th ed. CLSI Reference Methods vs Commercial Methods and CLSI vs US Food and Drug Administration Breakpoints It is important for users of M02,1 M07,2 and M100 to recognize that the standard methods described in CLSI documents are reference methods. These methods may be used for routine antimicrobial susceptibility testing of patient isolates, for evaluating commercial devices that will be used in medical laboratories, or by drug or device manufacturers for testing new agents or systems. Results generated by reference methods, such as those included in CLSI documents, may be used by regulatory authorities to evaluate the performance of commercial susceptibility testing devices as part of the approval process. Clearance by a regulatory authority indicates the commercial susceptibility testing device provides susceptibility results that are substantially equivalent to results generated using reference methods for the organisms and antimicrobial agents described in the device manufacturer’s approved package insert. CLSI breakpoints may differ from those approved by various regulatory authorities for many reasons, including use of different databases, differences in data interpretation, differences in doses used in different parts of the world, and public health policies. Differences also exist because CLSI proactively evaluates the need for changing breakpoints. The reasons why breakpoints may change and the manner in which CLSI evaluates data and determines breakpoints are outlined in CLSI document M23.5 Following a decision by CLSI to change an existing breakpoint, regulatory authorities may also review data to determine how changing breakpoints may affect the safety and effectiveness of the antimicrobial agent for the approved indications. If the regulatory authority changes breakpoints, commercial device manufacturers may have to conduct a clinical trial, submit the data to the regulatory authority, and await review and approval. For these reasons, a delay of one or more years may be needed if a breakpoint and interpretive category change is to be implemented by a device manufacturer. In the United States, it is acceptable for laboratories that use US Food and Drug Administration (FDA)– cleared susceptibility testing devices to use existing FDA breakpoints. Either FDA or CLSI susceptibility breakpoints are acceptable to laboratory accrediting organizations in the United States. Policies in other countries may vary. Each laboratory should check with the manufacturer of its antimicrobial susceptibility test system for additional information on the breakpoints and interpretive categories used in its system’s software. Following discussions with appropriate stakeholders (eg, infectious diseases and pharmacy practitioners, the pharmacy and therapeutics and infection prevention committees of the medical staff, and the antimicrobial stewardship team), newly approved or revised breakpoints may be implemented by laboratories. Following verification, CLSI disk diffusion test breakpoints may be implemented as soon as they are published in M100. If a device includes antimicrobial test concentrations sufficient to allow interpretation of susceptibility and resistance to an agent using the CLSI breakpoints, a laboratory could choose to, after appropriate verification, interpret and report results using CLSI breakpoints. xxxiii M100, 30th ed. Subcommittee on Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing Mission Statement The Subcommittee on Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing is composed of representatives from the professions, government, and industry, including microbiology laboratories, government agencies, health care providers and educators, and pharmaceutical and diagnostic microbiology industries. Using the CLSI voluntary consensus process, the subcommittee develops standards that promote accurate antimicrobial susceptibility testing and appropriate reporting. The mission of the Subcommittee on Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing is to:  Develop standard reference methods for antimicrobial susceptibility tests.  Provide quality control parameters for standard test methods.  Establish breakpoints and interpretive categories for the results of standard antimicrobial susceptibility tests and provide epidemiological cutoff values when breakpoints are not available.  Provide suggestions for testing and reporting strategies that are clinically relevant and cost-effective.  Continually refine standards and optimize detection of emerging resistance mechanisms through development of new or revised methods, breakpoints, and quality control parameters.  Educate users through multimedia communication of standards and guidelines.  Foster a dialogue with users of these methods and those who apply them. The ultimate purpose of the subcommittee’s mission is to provide useful information to enable laboratories to assist the clinician in the selection of appropriate antimicrobial therapy for patient care. The standards and guidelines are meant to be comprehensive and to include all antimicrobial agents for which the data meet established CLSI guidelines. The values that guide this mission are quality, accuracy, fairness, timeliness, teamwork, consensus, and trust. CLSI eCLIPSE - Dewanand Mahto - BD - 01/29/2020.
